content.full_text:
RAw MEHTA
CHAIRMAN
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
January24, 1997
John A. Miller
Director of Governmental Affairs
California Union of Safety Employees
2029 H Street
Sacramento, California 95814
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-96-362
Dear Mr. Miller:
This letter is a response to your request for advice regarding the provisions ofthe
Political Reform Act (the “Act”).’
QUESTIONS
1. Is the maximum a sponsoring organization can contribute to their sponsored small
contributor committee $500 a year, even though those funds or any additional amounts were
deposited into an operating account to exclusively pay overhead costs such as salaries or partial
salaries, office rent, telephones, printing, postage and mailing of newsletters, committee
solicitation materials and other non-candidate contribution responsibilities?
2. If a sponsored committee establishes an independent expenditure account, would the
sponsoring organization be limited to contributing a maximum of $50 annually toward the
overhead expenses and administrative costs described above?
3. Can the volunteer chair of a sponsored committee hold this position if he or she also
voluntarily chairs or serves on the sponsoring organization’s legislative committee which
provides direction to the sponsoring organization’s legislative advocate(s)? What if this same
person is compensated for overseeing legislative activities and is authorized to communicate
with state legislators, elected or state administrative employees?
Government Code sections 81000 - 91015. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18000
- 18995, of
the California Code of Regulations.
428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814
(916) 322-5660


File No. A-96-362
Page 2
4. Can the sponsored committee of an employer of lobbyists contribute to officeholder
accounts ifthe committee qualifies as a small contributor committee, and the committee’s
officeholder contributions are not approved by the sponsoring organization, nor does the
sponsoring organization have any control over the committee’s officeholder contributions other
than initially appointing the members of the committee’s governing committee?
CONCLUSIONS
1. The maximum a sponsoring organization may contribute to its sponsored small
contributor committee is $50 per year. The sponsoring organization may, however, provide
unlimited support to the committee for administration and establishment expenses.
2. The maximum a sponsoring organization may contribute to an independent
expenditure committee is $250 per election. The sponsoring organization may, however, provide
unlimited support to the committee for administration and establishment expenses.
3. The chair of a sponsored committee may hold the position if he or she provides
direction to the sponsoring organization’s legislative advocate, but if the chair is a registered
lobbyist, he or she may not make or arrange contributions to candidates or officeholders the chair
is registered to lobby.
4. The sponsored committee of a lobbyist employer may contribute to officeholder
accounts as long as the lobbyist employer does not have control over the committee’s
contributions other than initially appointing the members ofthe governing committee.
ANALYSIS
A recipient committee that is not candidate controlled and makes contributions to
candidates may not accept from any person a contribution or contributions totaling more than
$500 per calendar year. (Section 8530 1(d).) A small contributor committee may not qualify as a
small contributor connnittee if it receives from any person in a calendar year a contribution of
more than $50. (Section 85203.) A committee that makes independent expenditures may not
accept contributions over $250 per election. (Section 85500(b).) For the purposes of
contribution limits, if a sponsoring organization “established, fmanced, maintained, or
controlled” its committee, then contributions made by the organization and any of its committees
would be considered contributions made by a single person. (Section 85311.)
On January 21, 1997, the Commission adopted an emergency amendment to Regulation


File No. A-96-362
Page 3
18215, which defines the term “contribution.”
2 The amendment now provides that the term
contribution does not include, “[a] payment by a sponsoring organization for the establishment
and administration of a sponsored committee, provided such payments are reported.”
(Regulation 18215(c)(16).) Payments for establishment and administration are defined as the
costs of “office space, phones, salaries, utilities, supplies, legal and accounting fees, and other
expenses incurred in setting up and running a sponsored committee.” (Regulation 18215(c)(16).)
If these payments are made through a monetary contribution, the contribution must be made by
separate instrument. (Id.) Simply put, the sponsoring organization may pay the establishment
and administrative costs of the organization’s political committee, and such payments will not
count against the committee’s contribution limit, but the committee must report the payments as
contributions.
3 Further, if the sponsoring organization provides the committee with cash rather
than services, the organization must write a separate check. The sponsoring organization may
not mix political contributions and administrative support payments in the same check.
You asked ifthe chair ofthe sponsored committee may also serve on the sponsoring
organization’s legislative committee that provides direction to the sponsoring organization’s
legislative advocates. You also asked ifthe answer would be the same ifthe person was
compensated for overseeing legislative activities and was authorized to communicate with state
legislators or administrative employees. Section 85704 prohibits lobbyists from making or
arranging contributions to officeholders or candidates ifthe lobbyist “fmances, engages, or is
authorized to engage in lobbying the agency for which the candidate is seeking election or the
governmental agency of the officeholder.” Ifthe chair ofthe sponsored committee is a registered
lobbyist, the chair may not arrange contributions to an officeholder or candidate if the lobbyist is
registered to lobby the officeholder’s agency. Conversely, ifthe chair of the sponsored
committee is not a lobbyist, then the prohibition would not apply. To the extent that the chair
would interact with lobbyists, the prohibition would only apply ifthe lobbyists in the
organization actually made or arranged the contributions. Ifthe chair is not a lobbyist, and made
contributions independently of the lobbyists, the prohibition would not apply.
You also asked whether the sponsored committee of a lobbyist employer could give
contributions to an officeholder account ifthe contributions are not approved or controlled by the
lobbyist employer. You asked whether the answer would be the same if the lobbyist employer
2
The emergency regulation will be valid for 120 days from the date of adoption. The Commission may adopt the
amendments on a permanent basis before the regulation expires.
3
If the contribution of such costs is an “in-kind” contribution, such as the provision of free office space, it would be
reported on Schedule C. Monetary contributions of such payments would be reported on Schedule A. Be sure to
provide a notation on the entry explaining that the payment is for administrative services.


File No. A-96-362
Page 4
appointed the members ofthe committee’s governing committee. Section 85313(c) provides that
no officeholder may accept contributions to his or her officeholder account from, through, or
arranged by a registered lobbyist or lobbyist employer ifthe lobbyist or lobbyist employer is
authorized to engage in lobbying the officeholder’s agency. This prohibition does not extend to
the lobbyist employer’s committee ifthe lobbyist employer does not arrange the contributions.
The lobbyist employer may appoint the committee’s governing committee, but ifthe employer
actually exercises control over the contributions, then the contributions may be considered to
have been arranged by the lobbyist employer.
As noted above, please keep in mind that Section 85311 provides that all payments made
by a person “established, fmanced, maintained, or controlled” by any organization or group of
persons shall be considered to be made by a single person. Therefore, ifyour union establishes
committees that make contributions to candidates, contributions made by the committees may be
aggregated with contributions made by the union or by other committees formed by the union. If
you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel
£
By: Liane Randolph
Staff Counsel, Legal Division
